current_week_words.casual_usage: もの is your go-to casual word for 'thing' — 「それ、なんのもの？」(sore, nan no mono?) = "what's that thing?" It also hides inside everyday compounds you already half-know: たべもの (food) and のみもの (drink).
current_week_words.formal_usage: Stays largely the same in polite speech, but shows up in set phrases like 「おわすれものにごちゅういください」(o-wasuremono ni gochūi kudasai) = "please don't forget your belongings" — the announcement you'll hear on every train and in every shop.

current_week_words.translation: gushing about your romantic partner in a way that mildly annoys everyone listening
current_week_words.what_it_is: のろけ is what happens when someone can't stop bragging about how great their partner is — the lovestruck rambling that makes single friends roll their eyes. The verb のろける means to do this openly, and a のろけばなし (noroke-banashi) is a full 'lovey-dovey brag story' told at a dinner table. There's no single English word for it — 'gushing' comes close, but it misses the specifically romantic, faintly obnoxious-but-endearing social ritual that のろけ names.
current_week_words.example_en: I listened to him gush about his girlfriend.

similar_words.translation: to gush/brag fondly about one's partner (the verb form)
red-hot/head-over-heels (describing a couple who can't keep their hands off each other)
lovey-dovey (from English 'love love'; a couple visibly smitten with each other)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: Used in plain form in casual speech — 「どこいくの？」(Doko iku no?) means 'Where are you going?' You'll hear this constantly among friends.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Becomes 「いきます」(ikimasu) in polite form. 「どこへいきますか？」(Doko e ikimasu ka?) is the formal equivalent for acquaintances or work.

all_words_learned.translation: when you're not hungry, but you eat because your mouth is lonely

all_words_learned.notes: (also said "Ku" - but avoided since it can mean suffering)
most common in everyday speech. From English. Used for phone numbers, scores, temperatures

all_words_learned.casual_usage: Dropped straight into casual questions — 「いつくるの？」(Itsu kuru no?) means 'When are you coming?' It pairs naturally with verbs like いく and くる that you already know.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: いつ itself never changes form; only the verb after it shifts — 「いつきますか？」(Itsu kimasu ka?) is the polite equivalent.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: いま is everywhere in casual speech — 「いまなに？」(ima nani?) means 'What are you up to right now?' and 「いまいい？」(ima ii?) is the quick way to ask 'Is now a good time?' It drops naturally in front of almost any verb.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word itself doesn't change in formal settings — 「いまよろしいですか？」(ima yoroshii desu ka?) is the polite way to ask 'Is now okay?' Only the verb that follows shifts into ます/です form.

all_words_learned.what_it_is: いただきます is the expression Japanese people say — hands together, often eyes closed — before every single meal. Literally meaning 'I humbly receive,' it's directed at the food itself, the animals and plants that became it, the farmers who grew it, and the person who cooked it. There's no English equivalent because English-speaking cultures simply don't have this ritual baked into daily life.

similar_words.translation: thanks for the feast (said after eating)
what a waste (the feeling of guilt over wasting something precious)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: たべる is one of the first verbs you'll reach for every day. 「なにたべる？」(Nani taberu?) — 'What are you gonna eat?' — is a constant among friends. Pair it with もう and the past tense: 「もうたべた」(mou tabeta) = 'I already ate.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Shifts to 「たべます」(tabemasu) in polite contexts. At a restaurant or with someone you've just met: 「なにをたべますか？」(Nani o tabemasu ka?) — 'What would you like to eat?'

all_words_learned.casual_usage: たのしい pops up constantly as a standalone reaction — 「たのしい！」(Tanoshii!) just means 'This is fun!' You'll also hear it in past-tense questions: 「たのしかった？」(Tanoshikatta?) — 'Was it fun?'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: In polite contexts, the noun form 「たのしみ」(tanoshimi) often appears — 「たのしみにしています」(tanoshimi ni shite imasu) means 'I'm looking forward to it.' The adjective itself works fine in polite speech: 「たのしいですね」.
all_words_learned.example_jp: まいにちたのしいです。

all_words_learned.casual_usage: たかい is the adjective you'll fire off reflexively in Japan — 「たかい！」(Takai!) alone means 'That's expensive!' among friends. It doubles for height too: 「あのやまたかいね」(Ano yama takai ne) = 'That mountain's tall, huh?'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word itself doesn't change form — simply add です: 「これはたかいですね」(Kore wa takai desu ne) = 'This is expensive, isn't it.' You'll use this version in shops or with people you've just met.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: たくさん slots in right before verbs or after nouns with が — 「たくさんたべた！」(Takusan tabeta!) = 'I ate so much!' It's one of the first quantity words you'll naturally reach for; you can drop it almost anywhere abundance is the point.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word doesn't change form — only the verb that follows it shifts. 「たくさんいただきました」(Takusan itadakimashita) = 'I received a great deal' is perfectly polite. You'll also see 「たくさんのかた」(takusan no kata) in announcements to mean 'a great many people.'

all_words_learned.translation: raccoon dog; Japan's beloved folkloric shapeshifter
all_words_learned.what_it_is: Tanuki are real animals — raccoon dogs native to Japan — but in folklore they're cheerful shapeshifters who love to trick people, usually harmlessly. You'll see stone tanuki statues at the entrance of almost every izakaya and many shops: big belly, wide-brimmed hat, sake bottle in hand. They symbolize good luck, prosperity, and mischief in the friendliest possible way.

similar_words.translation: fox; the cunning, elegant shapeshifter of Japanese mythology
supernatural creature; the broader category tanuki belongs to
ghost or monster; a supernatural being that transforms or disguises itself

all_words_learned.casual_usage: おいしい！ (Oishii!) is what you'll exclaim after the first bite of something great — the single most useful thing you can say at any restaurant or dinner table in Japan. Flip it to past tense: おいしかった (oishikatta) = 'that was delicious' after finishing a meal.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Simply add です: おいしいですね (oishii desu ne) = 'This is delicious, isn't it?' is perfectly polite and will earn you smiles everywhere. Restaurant staff may say おいしゅうございます (oishuu gozaimasu), but you won't need that for a long time.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: おなかがすいた！(Onaka ga suita!) is the everyday way to say 'I'm hungry' — literally 'my stomach got empty.' You'll use this one daily. After a meal: おなかがいっぱい (onaka ga ippai) = 'I'm stuffed.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: おなか works just fine in most situations without adjustment — it's not rude even in restaurants or with new acquaintances. If you want to be extra polite: おなかがすきました (onaka ga sukimashita) for 'I'm hungry.'

similar_words.translation: throat (のどがかわいた = I'm thirsty)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: おはよう is the morning greeting for anyone you're close with — friends, family, teammates. Drop it the moment you see someone before noon. Stretch it warmly — おはよーう — for a lazy, friendly feel. Among tight friend groups you'll sometimes hear the ultra-casual おす (osu).
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Add ございます to get おはようございます (ohayou gozaimasu) — the default morning greeting in every Japanese workplace, school, and formal setting. You walk through the door, you say it. Everyone says it back. It's as automatic as breathing.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: おもしろい！(Omoshiroi!) is the instant reaction when something is entertaining, surprising, or just genuinely fun — it covers 'funny,' 'interesting,' and 'cool' all at once. You'll also hear 「おもしろそう！」(omoshirosou!) = 'That looks interesting/fun!' before you've even tried something.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word works fine in polite speech without changing — 「これはおもしろいですね」(Kore wa omoshiroi desu ne) = 'This is interesting, isn't it.' For academic or professional writing, 「きょうみぶかい」(kyoumibukiai) means 'intellectually interesting,' but おもしろい handles everyday formal conversation perfectly.
all_words_learned.example_jp: このえいがはおもしろい。

all_words_learned.translation: you must be tired; thanks for your hard work — Japan's universal end-of-effort greeting
all_words_learned.what_it_is: おつかれさま (or more formally おつかれさまでした) is said to anyone who has finished working, exerting effort, or gone through something hard. In Japanese offices it doubles as a daily greeting — you say it both arriving and leaving. There's no English equivalent because English simply doesn't have a social ritual built specifically to acknowledge someone's effort. The word literally means 'honorable tiredness.'

similar_words.translation: thanks for your trouble (said downward — boss to employee; using it to peers feels condescending)
casual short form — used among friends, in texts, and as a Gen Z internet greeting
good luck / hang in there (said BEFORE the effort — the opposite bookend)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: あしたなにするの？(Ashita nani suru no?) — 'What are you doing tomorrow?' — is one of the most natural casual questions you'll reach for every day. You'll also drop あしたまた (ashita mata) as a breezy sign-off meaning 'See you tomorrow.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: あした works fine in polite speech without changing form. あしたよろしくおねがいします (ashita yoroshiku onegai shimasu) = 'I'll be in your care tomorrow.' In written Japanese, the same word is often written as the kanji 明日 (still read ashita, or sometimes asu in more formal contexts).

all_words_learned.casual_usage: あつい！(Atsui!) is a reflex — you'll say it before you've finished stepping outside on a Japanese summer afternoon. 「きょうはあついね」(Kyou wa atsui ne) — 'It's hot today, huh?' — is Japan's single most universal small-talk opener, guaranteed to get nods from anyone within earshot.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word itself doesn't change form — just add です: 「きょうはあついですね」(Kyou wa atsui desu ne) = 'It's hot today, isn't it.' You'll say this version in offices and with new acquaintances, especially May through September when Japan's heat is inescapable.

similar_words.translation: cold to the touch (cold drinks, cold floors)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: あさ is the first word you reach for at the start of the day. 「あさごはんたべた？」(Asa gohan tabeta?) — 'Did you eat breakfast?' — flows out before you're even fully awake. It fuses naturally with あした (tomorrow, which you already know): あしたのあさ = 'tomorrow morning.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: あさ itself doesn't change form — only the verb and particles around it shift up. In formal settings you'll more often hear けさ (kesa) for 'this morning' specifically: 「けさはどうでしたか？」(Kesa wa dou deshita ka?) — 'How was this morning?' But あさ works fine for morning in general.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: あう is your default verb whenever you're getting together with someone. 「ともだちにあう」(Tomodachi ni au) — 'I'm meeting a friend' — or 「あとであおう！」(Ato de aou!) — 'Let's meet up later!' It slots in naturally with あした (tomorrow): あしたあおう = 'Let's meet tomorrow.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: It shifts to 「あいます」(aimasu) in polite speech. 「なんじにあいますか？」(Nanji ni aimasu ka?) — 'What time shall we meet?' — is the polite version for work or with new acquaintances. You'll also see お目にかかる (o-me ni kakaru) in highly formal situations, but あいます carries you everywhere you need to go.

similar_words.translation: a greeting; what you do the moment you meet someone
to gather together (a group meeting up)
to talk/speak (what naturally follows meeting someone)

all_words_learned.translation: to act sweetly dependent; to lean on someone's love and expect to be indulged
all_words_learned.what_it_is: あまえる describes the act of leaning into someone's affection — being a little babyish, clingy, or adorably needy with someone who loves you, fully expecting them to indulge you. It's not manipulation; it's the warm intimacy of letting your guard down with someone safe. A child whining softly to a parent, a partner being gently clingy, a cat pushing their head into your hand — all of that is あまえる.

similar_words.translation: sweet (the root adjective; 甘 meaning sweetness is where あまえる comes from)
restraint / holding back (the social opposite of あまえる)
to spoil / indulge someone (what you do when someone あまえる toward you)

all_words_learned.example_jp: あかちゃんがあまえる。

all_words_learned.casual_usage: きょう is one of those words you reach for every single day without thinking. 「きょうなにするの？」(Kyou nani suru no?) — 'What are you doing today?' — flows out naturally among friends. You can also drop it in front of words you already know: きょうもたのしい！= 'Today is fun too!'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: きょう works in any register — only the verb after it shifts polite. 「きょうはよろしくおねがいします」(Kyou wa yoroshiku onegai shimasu) = 'I'm in your care today.' In formal written Japanese, you'll see ほんじつ (honjitsu) as the elevated equivalent — in announcements or official documents — but きょう covers everything in spoken life.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: きのう is the natural backward-looking complement to きょう — it's in virtually every recap conversation. 「きのうなにしてたの？」(Kinou nani shiteta no?) = 'What were you doing yesterday?' You'll also drop it before verbs you already know: きのうたべた (kinou tabeta) = 'I ate it yesterday.'
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word itself never changes form — only the verbs that follow shift up. 「きのうはどうでしたか？」(Kinou wa dou deshita ka?) = 'How was yesterday?' is perfectly polite. In written Japanese, 昨日 is the kanji form (still read kinou, or sakujitsu in very formal contexts).

all_words_learned.casual_usage: きれい！(Kirei!) stands alone as an instant reaction — drop it on a view, a tidy room, or someone's handwriting. It covers both beauty and cleanliness in one word, so 「へやがきれいだね」(Heya ga kirei da ne) = 'The room is clean, huh?' works just as naturally as a compliment.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: きれい is a な-adjective, so polite speech just adds です: 「きれいですね」(kirei desu ne) = 'It's beautiful/clean, isn't it?' You'll use this phrase constantly — in restaurants, outside, meeting someone's home for the first time.

similar_words.translation: beautiful (more literary/poetic; for grand scenes or formal compliments)

all_words_learned.casual_usage: きもち pops up constantly in everyday speech, especially to describe how you feel in the moment. 「いいきもち！」(Ii kimochi!) = "What a great feeling!" — you might say it after a good stretch, a cool breeze, or finishing something satisfying. 「きもちわかるよ」(Kimochi wakaru yo) = "I get how you feel" — it's the go-to phrase for showing empathy between friends.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: In polite contexts the word itself stays the same, but you add です or soften the expression. 「おきもちはいかがですか」(Okimochi wa ikaga desu ka) = "How are you feeling?" — with the honorific お prefix, it becomes respectful and gentle, used toward someone who is unwell or has been through something difficult.

all_words_learned.translation: the heart-squeezing feeling from something unbearably cute or touching
all_words_learned.what_it_is: きゅんきゅん is a gitaigo — a word that mimics a sensation rather than a sound. It describes that tight, fluttery squeeze in your chest when something is so cute, sweet, or emotionally moving that it almost hurts. A puppy falling asleep in your hands, the moment two characters finally confess in a drama — your heart goes きゅん. In anime, manga, and Japanese social media, saying きゅんきゅんする (kyun-kyun suru) means your heart is being squeezed over and over again, helplessly.

similar_words.translation: heart pounding with excitement or nervousness
for the heart to flutter with excitement (the root of Marie Kondo's 'spark joy')
a chest-squeezing moment; heart going きゅん inside your chest (むね)

all_words_learned.example_en: My heart did a little squeeze.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: なまえは？ (namae wa?) is the ultra-casual way to ask someone's name — short and direct between friends. You'll also catch なまえおしえて (namae oshiete) = "Tell me your name" said without any particles at all.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Add the honorific お: おなまえは？ (onamae wa?) is what hotel staff, café clerks, and doctors reach for automatically. Full formal: おなまえをおしえていただけますか (onamae wo oshiete itadakemasu ka) = "Could you please tell me your name?"

all_words_learned.casual_usage: なんで is the gut-reaction question word you reach for any time something doesn't make sense. 「なんでいくの？」(Nande iku no?) = "Why are you going?" You'll also hear it trail off: 「なんでだろう…」(Nande darou...) = "I wonder why…"
all_words_learned.formal_usage: なんで is too casual for professional settings — swap it for なぜ (naze) or どうして (doushite). 「なぜそうしましたか？」(Naze sou shimashita ka?) = "Why did you do that?" is clean and appropriate anywhere.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: なる follows adjectives (in -ku form) and nouns + に to describe any change: あつくなる = it is getting hot, ともだちになる = to become friends. One of the most versatile words in casual Japanese — attach it to almost anything to say things are changing.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The polite form is なります (narimasu), heard constantly at shops and restaurants: こちらになります (kochira ni narimasu) = "Here you are / That will be...". Same meaning, just a polished ending — the core word stays identical.

similar_words.translation: to become able to; can do

all_words_learned.casual_usage: なかに入って！(Naka ni haitte!) = "Come inside!" flows naturally between friends. When someone asks where your keys are, かばんのなか (kaban no naka) = "inside the bag" is the instant answer.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: なか itself is register-neutral and does not change in polite speech. The formality is carried by surrounding verbs and endings: …のなかにあります (no naka ni arimasu) is perfectly polite.

all_words_learned.translation: the pleasant feeling of relaxing at a slow, unhurried pace; leisurely ease
all_words_learned.what_it_is: まったり is a gitaigo (mimetic word) that describes a state of comfortable, unhurried relaxation — like melting into a couch on a Sunday with nowhere to be. It captures a distinctly Japanese appreciation for luxurious idleness, a conscious savoring of slowness. The word also doubles as a food descriptor, used to describe rich, smooth, full-bodied flavors.
all_words_learned.example_en: I just want to take it easy.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: りんごたべる？(Ringo taberu?) = 'Want to eat an apple?' flows naturally between friends. Pair it with juice: りんごジュース (ringo jūsu) is one of the most common drink orders in Japan.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: The word doesn't change form in formal settings — just adjust what surrounds it. りんごをいただきました (ringo wo itadakimashita) = 'I received an apple' is perfectly polite when thanking someone for a gift.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: Between friends you'd say 「りょうりする？」(wanna cook?) or compliment someone with 「このりょうり、うまい！」(this food is amazing!). It covers both the act of cooking and the resulting dish.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Add the polite お prefix: 「おりょうりはいかがですか？」(How is the cuisine?) is standard when hosting guests or in a restaurant setting. Restaurants often use 「本日のおりょうり」(today's dishes).

all_words_learned.casual_usage: ひとり shows up constantly for solo activities — ひとりで食べた (hitori de tabeta = "I ate alone") or ひとりじゃない (hitori ja nai = "you're not alone") are everyday phrases between friends.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: In service settings, おひとりですか (ohitori desu ka = "Just one?") is the standard restaurant greeting. The word stays the same — just add おー and polite endings.

all_words_learned.translation: that warm, fuzzy feeling of being wrapped in emotional coziness or heartwarming contentment
all_words_learned.what_it_is: ほっこり is a gitaigo — a word that mimics a sensation — capturing the feeling of warmth spreading through you when something is genuinely cozy, comforting, or quietly touching. It's the feeling of holding a hot cup of tea in both hands while it rains outside, of watching a cat curl into a sunbeam, or hearing exactly the right kind words at exactly the right moment. 'Cozy' gets partway there but misses the emotional warmth; 'heartwarming' is close but too dramatic. ほっこり is quieter — the small, real, everyday kind.

similar_words.translation: soft, warm, and gently hazy; more about atmosphere than feeling
to feel soothed and settle into warmth; the verb version of this sensation
blissfully unbothered, floating through life without a care

all_words_learned.casual_usage: のむ flows naturally in everyday conversation — 「なにのむ？」(Nani nomu?) = "What are you drinking?" is one of the first questions you will ask or be asked any day. Pair it with たべる (already learned): 「たべてのんで、しあわせ！」= "Eating and drinking, I am happy!"
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Shifts to のみます (nomimasu) in polite speech — 「なにをのみますか？」(Nani o nomimasu ka?) = "What would you like to drink?" is what every restaurant and café server will ask you. You will also hear 「おのみものはいかがですか？」= "Would you care for a drink?"

all_words_learned.casual_usage: のど comes up constantly — say のどがかわいた (nodo ga kawaita) when you're thirsty (literally 'my throat dried out'), or のどがいたい (nodo ga itai) for a sore throat.
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Same word in polite speech. You might say のどのぐあいがわるいです (nodo no guai ga warui desu) — "my throat is not well" — in a formal or medical context.

similar_words.translation: to dry out / to be thirsty

all_words_learned.translation: to ride; to get on (a vehicle)
all_words_learned.casual_usage: のる is what you say when hopping on trains, buses, bikes, or even elevators — 「でんしゃにのる」(densha ni noru) = "to ride the train." Friends might just ask 「なんでいくの？のる？あるく？」= "How are you getting there? Riding or walking?"
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Becomes のります (norimasu) in polite speech — 「なんじのでんしゃにのりますか？」(Nanji no densha ni norimasu ka?) = "What time train will you take?" is a natural question when making plans with someone you're not close to.

all_words_learned.casual_usage: もの is your go-to casual word for 'thing' — 「それ、なんのもの？」(sore, nan no mono?) = "what's that thing?" It also hides inside everyday compounds you already half-know: たべもの (food) and のみもの (drink).
all_words_learned.formal_usage: Stays largely the same in polite speech, but shows up in set phrases like 「おわすれものにごちゅういください」(o-wasuremono ni gochūi kudasai) = "please don't forget your belongings" — the announcement you'll hear on every train and in every shop.

all_words_learned.translation: gushing about your romantic partner in a way that mildly annoys everyone listening
all_words_learned.what_it_is: のろけ is what happens when someone can't stop bragging about how great their partner is — the lovestruck rambling that makes single friends roll their eyes. The verb のろける means to do this openly, and a のろけばなし (noroke-banashi) is a full 'lovey-dovey brag story' told at a dinner table. There's no single English word for it — 'gushing' comes close, but it misses the specifically romantic, faintly obnoxious-but-endearing social ritual that のろけ names.
all_words_learned.example_en: I listened to him gush about his girlfriend.

similar_words.translation: to gush/brag fondly about one's partner (the verb form)
red-hot/head-over-heels (describing a couple who can't keep their hands off each other)
lovey-dovey (from English 'love love'; a couple visibly smitten with each other)

user_words.translation: when you're not hungry, but you eat because your mouth is lonely

user_words.notes: (also said "Ku" - but avoided since it can mean suffering)
most common in everyday speech. From English. Used for phone numbers, scores, temperatures

notes: Neosai state file. Routines read and write this. user_words is the manually-added list (replaces user-lists.json from v1). all_words_learned grows over time and includes both routine-delivered words and user-added words distinguished by the 'source' field.
